1: הָבוּ לַיהוָה בְּנֵי אֵלִים (hābû laYHWH bĕnê ʾêlîm, "Ascribe to the LORD, O heavenly beings") — bĕnê ʾêlîm: "sons of God/divine beings" — the heavenly council that surrounds the divine throne (Job 1:6; Ps 82:1, 6). The psalm opens by commanding the divine council to worship YHWH — not Israel, but the celestial assembly itself. This is a remarkable claim about YHWH's supremacy in the heavenly realm.
2: הָבוּ לַיהוָה כְּבוֹד שְׁמוֹ (hābû laYHWH kĕbôd shĕmô, "Give the LORD the glory due his name") — kābôd: weight, honor, splendor — the overwhelming presence of God. הִשְׁתַּחֲווּ לַיהוָה בְּהַדְרַת-קֹדֶשׁ (hishtaḥăwû laYHWH bĕhadrat-qōdesh, "Worship the LORD in the splendor of his holiness") — hadrat-qōdesh: a debated phrase; "splendor/glory of holiness" or "holy attire/array." Either way, worship is not casual but appropriately clothed in what holiness requires.
3: קוֹל יְהוָה עַל-הַמָּיִם (qôl YHWH ʿal-hammāyim, "The voice of the LORD is over the waters") — the first of seven qôl YHWH (Voice of the LORD) occurrences in vv3-9, functioning as a liturgical refrain. אֵל-הַכָּבוֹד הִרְעִים (ʾêl-hakkābôd hirʿîm, "the God of glory thunders") — ʾêl hakkābôd: "the God of glory" — a unique phrase in the Psalter. Stephen cites it in Acts 7:2 ("The God of glory appeared to our father Abraham"). The title identifies YHWH as the possessor of the divine kābôd (weight/presence) that the divine council was commanded to ascribe to him.
4: קוֹל-יְהוָה בַּכֹּחַ (qôl-YHWH bakkōaḥ, "The voice of the LORD is powerful") — kōaḥ: strength, force, power — the raw physical energy of the divine voice (thunder). קוֹל יְהוָה בֶּהָדָר (qôl YHWH behādār, "the voice of the LORD is majestic") — hādār: splendor, majesty, beauty — the aesthetic dimension alongside brute force. These two terms (power + majesty) form the Ugaritic storm-god Baal's vocabulary, here entirely reassigned to YHWH — the theological polemic is embedded in the word-choice.
5: קוֹל יְהוָה שֹׁבֵר אֲרָזִים (qôl YHWH shōbêr ʾărāzîm, "The voice of the LORD shatters the cedars") — shābar: to shatter, to break into pieces. The cedars of Lebanon (ʾarzê hallĕbānôn) were the largest, most permanent trees in the ancient world — symbols of royal power and permanence (Isa 2:13; Ezek 31:3). If the divine voice shatters what no human force can break, it is omnipotent.
6: וַיַּרְקִידֵם כְּמוֹ-עֵגֶל (wayyarqîdêm kĕmô-ʿêgel, "He makes Lebanon skip like a calf") — rāqad: to skip, to dance, to leap (the joy-dance). The mountain-shattering force that breaks cedars causes the mountains to dance like calves. Sîryôn (Sirion): the Phoenician name for Mount Hermon (Deut 3:9). The wild ox (rĕʾêm) was the most uncontrollable animal known in the ancient world — yet it "skips" before the divine voice.
7: קוֹל יְהוָה חֹצֵב לַהֲבוֹת אֵשׁ (qôl YHWH ḥōṣêb lahăbôt ʾêsh, "The voice of the LORD strikes with flashes of lightning") — ḥāṣab: to hew, to cut out, to carve — the lightning as if chiseled from the storm. Lahăbôt ʾêsh: "flames of fire" — lightning as fire-flames cut from the sky. The creative/destructive ambiguity: the same verb used for stonecutting is used for the lightning-bolt, as if God is a craftsman cutting fire from the sky.
8: קוֹל יְהוָה יָחִיל מִדְבָּר (qôl YHWH yāḥîl midbār, "The voice of the LORD shakes the wilderness") — ḥûl: to writhe, to tremble, to be in labor — the same verb used in v9 for the deer writhing in labor. The wilderness (midbār) itself writhes at the divine voice. Midbār qādesh: the wilderness of Kadesh — the southern region through which Israel wandered, now the scene of theophanic power.
9: קוֹל יְהוָה יְחוֹלֵל אַיָּלוֹת (qôl YHWH yĕḥôlêl ʾayyālôt, "The voice of the LORD causes the deer to writhe in labor") — yāḥal (polel: to cause to writhe/bring to birth): the divine voice triggers labor contractions in the deer. The same voice that breaks cedars also determines the moment of birthing — control over both the large and the small. וּבְהֵיכָלוֹ כֻּלּוֹ אֹמֵר כָּבוֹד (ûbĕhêkālô kullô ʾōmêr kābôd, "in his temple all cry, 'Glory!'") — the seventh and final qôl YHWH produces the worshiping cry: kābôd (glory). The heavenly assembly responds with the one word that encapsulates all ascription.
10: יְהוָה לַמַּבּוּל יָשָׁב (YHWH lammabbûl yāshāb, "The LORD sat enthroned over the Flood") — mabbûl: the Noahic flood — the only use of this specific word in the Psalter. It is the exclusive word Genesis uses for Noah's flood (Gen 6-9), not the generic flood-word (shéṭeph). YHWH was enthroned above the primordial chaos-flood — the creator was not threatened but in sovereign session above it. וַיֵּשֶׁב יְהוָה מֶלֶךְ לְעוֹלָם (wayyêsheb YHWH melek lĕʿôlām, "the LORD sits enthroned as King forever") — the one who sat above the primordial flood is the one who sits forever.
11: יְהוָה עֹז לְעַמּוֹ יִתֵּן (YHWH ʿōz lĕʿammô yittên, "The LORD gives strength to his people") — the same ʿōz (strength) that the heavenly beings were commanded to ascribe to YHWH (v1) is now given by YHWH to his people. The divine strength is not hoarded but distributed. יְהוָה יְבָרֵךְ אֶת-עַמּוֹ בַשָּׁלוֹם (YHWH yĕbārêk ʾet-ʿammô bashshālôm, "the LORD blesses his people with peace") — the storm-theophany psalm ends with shālôm (peace, wholeness). The God whose voice shatters cedars and triggers labor blesses his people with peace. The power is the foundation of the peace: only the one mightier than every storm can give true shālôm.
